2	name: progress-critic
3	description: Fresh-context audit of recent iteration progress per active file/route. Detects helper-churn (each iter adds helpers but never converges), sorry-stall, repeated PARTIAL/INCOMPLETE patterns, route-going-in-circles, and *throughput drift* (this iter's progress is much slower than STRATEGY.md's estimate predicts). Also checks the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal for dispatch-sanity issues (excessive objective count, files known to be blocked). Renders a CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR verdict per active route with specific corrective recommendations when CHURNING or STUCK.

10	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
11	    do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
12	    have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
13	    verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.
14	
15	    **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
16	      - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
17	        or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
18	        skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
19	      - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
20	        signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
21	        added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
22	        CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
23	      - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
24	        sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
25	        is no trajectory to extrapolate from).

27	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
28	    with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
29	    open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
30	    re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
31	  - My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
32	    progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
33	    The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
34	    judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
35	    corrective.

37	  ### Strict context discipline
38	
39	  Your directive must contain ONLY:
40	    - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
41	      iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
42	    - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
43	      sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
44	      (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.

46	      and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —

48	      K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
49	      `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
50	      whole strategy.
51	    - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for

54	    - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.

57	    - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
58	      soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
59	    - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
60	      reviewer's territory).
61	    - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
62	      above).
63	
64	  If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
65	  it. My value depends on the narrow focus.
66	
67	  ### Acting on my verdicts
68	
69	  Verdicts are per-route:
70	
71	  - **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
72	    prover round.
73	  - **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
74	    shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
75	    corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
76	    structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the

79	    iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.

81	    data). Proceed but watch.

89	
90	# Progress Critic
91	
92	You are the fresh-context progress critic. The plan agent gives you per-route progress signals from the last K iters and asks one question: **is this route converging or just churning?**
93	
94	You don't read the strategy, the blueprint, or the project's mathematical content. Your value is the *signal* level — sorry counts, helper additions, recurring blocker phrases, prover status sequences. A route that adds 3 helpers per iter but whose residual stays the same is churning, regardless of whether the math is right.
95	
96	## Stance
97	
98	You are the corrective for a known failure pattern. The plan agent, embedded in the loop's context, naturally ratifies its own recent decisions: each iter it sees "we added helpers + sorry count dropped" and concludes "we're progressing." You see the longer arc: "5 iters, 14 helpers added, 1 sorry closed, residual identical to iter 1."
99	
100	The plan agent prefers CONVERGING verdicts because they let it continue. You should NOT give that bias the benefit of the doubt. When the signals point at churn, you say CHURNING. When the signals point at stall, you say STUCK. You don't soften.
101	
102	**The plan agent's laziness is also a signal.** The following planner behaviors are themselves churn patterns — they are not "good planning" and must not be treated as structural progress:
103	
104	- Dispatching a single prover when multiple files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries are available and could be worked on at the same time. Under-loaded dispatch is artificial throttling and burns iterations.
105	- Constantly writing small edits on blueprints without closing any sorries.
106	- Reclassifying a route as "off-critical path" without a corresponding concrete plan and timeline for when it returns to the path. "Off-critical path" with no re-engagement plan is indefinite deferral.
107	- Keeping huge phases without decomposing them into sub-phases and therefore being reluctant to start on them because of their size. 
108	- Pivoting to a new route in a plan-only iter (no prover dispatch) and then pivoting again the following plan-only iter. Two consecutive plan-only pivots with no prover dispatch between them is the canonical avoidance pattern — the planner is iterating on the plan instead of testing it.
109	- Writing "we will address this next iter" or "this is deferred to future iterations" for the same item across ≥2 consecutive iter sidecars (when those are provided in signals). Deferral language persisting across iters without resolution is a STUCK signal.
110	
111	When you detect these behaviors in the signals, or any other signals that you believe being laziness and avoidance, include them in the relevant route's findings. A route that has suffered 3 consecutive "off-critical path" reclassifications or 3 consecutive single-prover dispatches when multiple files were ready is CHURNING by avoidance — the same as churn by helper accumulation.
112

190	
191	## What you check
192	
193	For each route's block:
194	
195	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
196	
197	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
198	
199	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
200	
201	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
202	
203	5. **Under-dispatch pattern.** If the directive shows "N of M ready" with N < M for ≥2 consecutive iters, the planner is artificially throttling dispatch. This is churn by avoidance — not a prover problem but a planning problem. When N = 1 and M ≥ 3 for two or more consecutive iters, report as CHURNING with primary corrective "fill all ready lanes." Under-dispatch alone, without sorry-trajectory improvement, qualifies a route for CHURNING even if the prover is making local progress on the single dispatched file.
204	
205	6. **Avoidance patterns.** Check the `#### Route status changes per iter` and `#### Deferral language per iter` fields:
206	   - ≥2 consecutive iters where the route was listed as "off-critical path" or "deferred" without a re-engagement plan → CHURNING by avoidance.
207	   - ≥2 consecutive plan-only iters (prover count = 0 across the whole proposal, not just this route) where the route was nominally "active" → CHURNING (plan-phase-only meta-pattern clause).
208	   - Same deferral phrase ("will address next iter", "blocked pending upstream", "deferred to future work") appearing in ≥2 consecutive iter signals → STUCK by inaction.
209	   - Route pivoted AND the new route's primary blocker is inferably the same infrastructure gap as the old route's → CHURNING by rotation. (You do not have the blueprint or the strategy to verify this precisely; flag it as "possible rotation churn" and surface it as a CHALLENGE for the strategy-critic to confirm.)
210	
211	7. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
212	
213	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent.
214	   - **Under-dispatch against ready files**: if the proposal lists fewer files than the "Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)" field shows are available, flag the gap explicitly. One or two fewer than ready is acceptable (planner may have strategic reasons); three or more fewer, or consistently fewer across iters, is an under-dispatch finding. Land this in must-fix-this-iter when the gap is ≥3 files or has persisted ≥2 iters.
215	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating.
216	
217	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report.
218	
219	8. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
220	
221	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
222	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
223	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
224	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
225	
226	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature. "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy.
227	
228	## Verdict rules
229	
230	Apply these rules verbatim:
231	
232	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND no under-dispatch pattern AND no avoidance pattern AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
233	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
234	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
235	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
236	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with zero prover dispatches on this route (no file ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds without ever firing a prover is textbook stall.
237	  - **under-dispatch pattern**: prover count = 1 (or otherwise N < M ready) for ≥2 consecutive iters with no sorry-trajectory improvement attributable to the dispatch strategy (i.e. the filed sorry on the dispatched lane didn't close either).
238	  - **avoidance pattern**: ≥2 consecutive iters with "off-critical path" / "deferred" route status AND no re-engagement plan in the proposal.
239	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters. OR: same deferral phrase persisting across ≥2 consecutive iters.
240	- **UNCLEAR**: route is fresh (< K iters of data) OR signals are ambiguous.
241	
242	If multiple rules match a route, pick the worse verdict (CHURNING > CONVERGING; STUCK > CHURNING).
243	
244	## Recommended actions per verdict
245	
246	For CHURNING or STUCK, your report names ONE primary corrective TYPE. The planner consults the catalog for the matching subagent.
247	
248	- **Blueprint expansion** — the chapter's proof sketch is likely under-specified; the planner should expand it before more prover work.
249	- **Mathlib analogy consult** — the project may be using a parallel API or wrong predicate; the planner should consult Mathlib-idiom analysis on the route's load-bearing definitions.
250	- **Refactor** — the definition or file structure may be wrong; dispatch a structural subagent before more prover work.
251	- **Route pivot** — the strategic route may be wrong entirely; revise STRATEGY.md and pick a different route, then re-run any strategy critic mid-iter to validate.
252	- **Fill all ready lanes** — the planner is under-dispatching; all files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries should be in `## Current Objectives` this iter, up to the dispatch cap.
253	- **Address deferred infrastructure** — the route has been marked off-critical-path or deferred; the planner must either write the blueprint chapter and open a prover lane this iter, or explicitly close the route as out-of-scope (which requires updating `## Goal` in STRATEGY.md if the goal depends on it).
254	- **User escalation** — none of the above will work; pause and request user input. Use sparingly — only when no automated corrective will resolve the stall.
255	
256	Pick ONE primary corrective per CHURNING/STUCK route. Multiple are allowed when truly necessary, listed in priority order.
257	
258	## Report format
259	
260	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/progress-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
261	
262	**Omit-empty rule.** Only `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict` are required. Omit any section whose right answer is "nothing to report." Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is CONVERGING with no recurring blockers, no avoidance patterns, and no secondary correctives, the block may be the trajectory + status pattern + verdict line only. Same for dispatch sanity: if dispatch is OK and no under-dispatch finding exists, render as a single line (`Verdict: OK — file count <N> within cap <C>, no under-dispatch`).

333	
334	- **You don't read strategy or blueprint.** Convergence is the question; soundness is for other subagents.
335	- **No bias toward CONVERGING.** The planner wants the route to be CONVERGING; you do not. Apply the verdict rules verbatim.
336	- **Under-dispatch is churn.** Sending one prover when three files are ready burns iterations just as surely as adding helpers that don't close sorries. Treat it as a planning failure, not a scheduling preference.
337	- **Avoidance is churn.** "Off-critical path" reclassification, consecutive plan-only iters, and persistent deferral language are all CHURNING signals. They indicate the planner is iterating around the hard problem instead of through it.

1	# Archon Project

3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple

41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 8	/-!
9	# Stalk of a tensor product of presheaves of modules (ingredient d.2)
10	
11	This file constructs the *comparison map* underlying the varying-ring stalk--tensor
12	commutation isomorphism
13	
14	  `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x ≅ A.stalk x ⊗_{R.stalk x} B.stalk x`
15	
16	for presheaves of modules over the structure presheaf `R` of a scheme — the single
17	genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient ("d.2") on which the unconditional associator of
18	the relative-Picard tensor substrate rests.
19	
20	This iteration builds the forward additive comparison map `stalkTensorDesc` and its
21	germ characterisation (the colimit-descent of the sectionwise base-change maps). The
22	full isomorphism `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso` (blueprint `lem:stalk_tensor_commutation`)
23	additionally requires the `R.stalk x`-linear packaging of this map, the reverse map, and
24	their mutual inversion on germs — see the handoff in `task_results`.
25	
26	The construction proceeds bottom-up:
27	
28	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorBilin` — for a neighbourhood `U ∋ x`, the `R(U)`-balanced
29	  bilinear map `A(U) × B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`, `(a,b) ↦ germ a ⊗ germ b`.
30	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDescU` — its descent through `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`
31	  to a map `(A ⊗ᵖ B)(U) = A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`.
32	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDesc` — the colimit descent to the comparison map
33	  `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`.
34	
35	Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma `lemma-stalk-tensor-product`.
36	
37	Blueprint: `chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`, §`sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor`
38	(`lem:stalk_tensor_commutation`).
39	-/
40	
41	set_option autoImplicit false
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory TopologicalSpace TopCat.Presheaf Opposite
46	
47	namespace PresheafOfModules
48	
49	open PresheafOfModules.Monoidal
50	
51	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — stalk of a tensor product (d.2) -/
52	
53	section StalkTensor
54	
55	variable {X : TopCat.{u}} {R : X.Presheaf CommRingCat.{u}}
56	  (A B : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) (x : X)
57	
58	local notation3 "Tgt" =>
59	  TensorProduct ↑(R.stalk x) ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk A.presheaf x)
60	    ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x)
61	
62	/-- **The `R(U)`-balanced bilinear comparison map.** For a neighbourhood `U ∋ x`, the
63	bilinear (additive-monoid-hom) map `A(U) × B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x` sending
64	`(a, b)` to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. Project-local: the building block of the d.2 stalk--tensor
65	comparison; Mathlib has no varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation. -/
66	noncomputable def stalkTensorBilin (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
67	    (↑(A.obj (op U)) : Type u) →+ (↑(B.obj (op U)) : Type u) →+ Tgt where
68	  toFun m :=
69	    ((TensorProduct.mk ↑(R.stalk x) _ _
70	      ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) m)).toAddMonoidHom).comp
71	      (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx))
72	  map_zero' := by ext n; simp
73	  map_add' m m' := by ext n; simp
74	
75	/-- The defining balancing identity making `stalkTensorBilin` descend through the
76	relative tensor product `A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U)`: `(r • a) ⊗ b ↦ a ⊗ (r • b)`. -/
77	lemma stalkTensorBilin_balanced (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) (r : ↑(R.obj (op U)))
78	    (m : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (n : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
79	    (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx (r • m)) n = (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx m) (r • n) := by
80	  simp only [stalkTensorBilin, AddMonoidHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk, AddMonoidHom.coe_comp,
81	    LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, Function.comp_apply, TensorProduct.mk_apply]
82	  rw [PresheafOfModules.germ_smul A x U hx]
83	  erw [PresheafOfModules.germ_smul B x U hx]
84	  exact TensorProduct.smul_tmul _ _ _
85	
86	/-- **The per-neighbourhood descended comparison map** `(A ⊗ᵖ B)(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`,
87	obtained from `stalkTensorBilin` via `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`. The source object is
88	`(A ⊗ᵖ B).presheaf.obj (op U)`, definitionally `A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U)`. -/
89	noncomputable def stalkTensorDescU (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
90	    (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf.obj (op U) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt :=
91	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
92	    (TensorProduct.liftAddHom (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx) (stalkTensorBilin_balanced A B x U hx))
93	
94	@[simp] lemma stalkTensorDescU_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
95	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
96	    (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b)
97	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
98	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b := by
99	  change (TensorProduct.liftAddHom (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx)
100	    (stalkTensorBilin_balanced A B x U hx)) (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b) = _
101	  rw [TensorProduct.liftAddHom_tmul]
102	  rfl
103	
104	/-- **Scalar compatibility of the per-neighbourhood descent.** For `r : R(U)` and a
105	section `z` of `A ⊗ᵖ B` over `U`, the descent `stalkTensorDescU` carries the `R(U)`-scalar
106	action over to the `R.stalk x`-action by the germ of `r`. This is stage (iii)'s
107	section-level core; the carrier-duality between the `CommRingCat` scalar `r : R(U)` and the
108	`RingCat`-carrier module structure on `A(U) ⊗ B(U)` is absorbed by `germ_smul`/`smul_tmul'`. -/
109	lemma stalkTensorDescU_smul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) (r : ↑(R.obj (op U)))
110	    (z : ↑((Monoidal.tensorObj A B).obj (op U))) :
111	    (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) (r • z)
112	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ R U x hx)) r •
113	          (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) z := by
114	  -- The section tensor `A(U) ⊗ B(U)` is a module over the `RingCat` carrier
115	  -- `S := (R ⋙ forget₂).obj (op U)`, which is defeq to the `CommRingCat` carrier `R(U)`.
116	  -- We pin `smul_tmul'`/`smul_add`/`smul_zero` to that ring so the scalar `r` slots in.
117	  induction z using TensorProduct.induction_on with
118	  | zero =>
119	      simp only [presheaf_obj_coe, Functor.comp_obj, CommRingCat.forgetToRingCat_obj,
120	        Monoidal.tensorObj_obj, map_zero, smul_zero]
121	      exact map_zero _
122	  | tmul a b =>
123	      erw [stalkTensorDescU_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul, PresheafOfModules.germ_smul A x U hx]
124	      exact (TensorProduct.smul_tmul' _ _ _).symm
125	  | add p q hp hq =>
126	      erw [smul_add]
127	      refine (map_add _ _ _).trans ?_
128	      rw [hp, hq, ← smul_add]
129	      exact congrArg _ (map_add _ p q).symm

131	/-- **The d.2 comparison map** `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`, obtained by descending
132	the per-neighbourhood maps `stalkTensorDescU` through the colimit defining the stalk. -/
133	noncomputable def stalkTensorDesc :
134	    TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt :=
135	  colimit.desc ((OpenNhds.inclusion x).op ⋙ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf)
136	    ⟨AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt,
137	      fun U => stalkTensorDescU A B x (unop U).1 (unop U).2,
138	      by
139	        intro U V i
140	        apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
141	        ext z
142	        induction z using TensorProduct.induction_on with
143	        | zero => exact (AddMonoidHom.map_zero _).trans (AddMonoidHom.map_zero _).symm
144	        | tmul a b =>
145	            simp only [CategoryTheory.comp_apply, Functor.const_obj_map]
146	            erw [tensorObj_map_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul]
147	            erw [germ_res_apply, germ_res_apply]
148	            rfl
149	        | add p q hp hq =>
150	            exact (AddMonoidHom.map_add _ p q).trans
151	              ((congrArg₂ HAdd.hAdd hp hq).trans (AddMonoidHom.map_add _ p q).symm)⟩
152	
153	/-- The comparison map `stalkTensorDesc` factors the per-neighbourhood map
154	`stalkTensorDescU` through the germ, by the colimit universal property. -/
155	@[reassoc]
156	lemma germ_stalkTensorDesc (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
157	    TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx ≫ stalkTensorDesc A B x
158	      = stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx :=
159	  colimit.ι_desc _ (op (⟨U, hx⟩ : OpenNhds x))
160	
161	/-- **Germ characterisation of the d.2 comparison map** on a simple tensor: the germ of
162	`a ⊗ b` over `U` maps to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. -/
163	@[simp]
164	lemma stalkTensorDesc_germ_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
165	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
166	    (stalkTensorDesc A B x)
167	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx))
168	          (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b))
169	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
170	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b := by
171	  rw [← stalkTensorDescU_tmul, ← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, germ_stalkTensorDesc]
172	
173	/-- **Element form of `germ_stalkTensorDesc`.** The comparison map sends the germ of a
174	section `w` over `U` to its per-neighbourhood descent `stalkTensorDescU`. -/
175	lemma stalkTensorDesc_germ (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
176	    (w : ↑((Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf.obj (op U))) :
177	    (stalkTensorDesc A B x)
178	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx)) w)
179	      = (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) w := by
180	  rw [← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, germ_stalkTensorDesc]
181	
182	/-- **The `R.stalk x`-linear d.2 comparison map** (stage (iii)). The additive comparison
183	map `stalkTensorDesc` is `R.stalk x`-linear for the stalk module structures of
184	`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk`: linearity is checked on a germ `germ r` of a
185	scalar `r : R(U)` via the germ--scalar compatibility `germ_smul` and the section-level
186	scalar compatibility `stalkTensorDescU_smul`, after passing to a common neighbourhood.
187	This is the linear packaging the d.2 stalk--tensor isomorphism `stalkTensorIso` builds on. -/
188	noncomputable def stalkTensorLinearMap :
189	    (↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x) : Type u)
190	      →ₗ[↑(R.stalk x)] Tgt where
191	  toFun := ConcreteCategory.hom (stalkTensorDesc A B x)
192	  map_add' a b := map_add _ a b
193	  map_smul' r ξ := by
194	    dsimp only [RingHom.id_apply]
195	    obtain ⟨U, hxU, r₀, rfl⟩ := TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist R x r
196	    obtain ⟨V, hxV, z, rfl⟩ :=
197	      TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x ξ
198	    set W : Opens X := U ⊓ V with hW
199	    have hxW : x ∈ W := ⟨hxU, hxV⟩
200	    set iWU : W ⟶ U := homOfLE inf_le_left
201	    set iWV : W ⟶ V := homOfLE inf_le_right
202	    have hr : (ConcreteCategory.hom (R.germ U x hxU)) r₀
203	        = (ConcreteCategory.hom (R.germ W x hxW)) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map iWU.op)) r₀) :=
204	      (TopCat.Presheaf.germ_res_apply R iWU x hxW r₀).symm
205	    have hz : (ConcreteCategory.hom
206	          (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf V x hxV)) z
207	        = (ConcreteCategory.hom
208	            (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf W x hxW))
209	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom ((Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf.map iWV.op)) z) :=
210	      (TopCat.Presheaf.germ_res_apply (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf iWV x hxW z).symm
211	    rw [hr, hz, ← PresheafOfModules.germ_smul (Monoidal.tensorObj A B) x W hxW,
212	      stalkTensorDesc_germ, stalkTensorDescU_smul, ← stalkTensorDesc_germ]
213	
214	/-- **Germ characterisation of the linear comparison map** on a simple tensor: the germ of
215	`a ⊗ b` over `U` maps to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. The `R.stalk x`-linear repackaging
216	`stalkTensorLinearMap` agrees with `stalkTensorDesc` on germs. -/
217	@[simp]
218	lemma stalkTensorLinearMap_germ_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
219	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
220	    stalkTensorLinearMap A B x
221	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx))
222	          (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b))
223	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
224	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b :=
225	  stalkTensorDesc_germ_tmul A B x U hx a b

227	/-! ### Stage (iv): the reverse map -/
228	
229	/-- **Inner cocone leg for the reverse map** (fixed `A`-section `a` over `U`). For a
230	`B`-neighbourhood `V ∋ x`, the additive map `B(V) → (A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x` sending `b` to the
231	germ of `(a|_{U⊓V}) ⊗ (b|_{U⊓V})`. Project-local building block of the d.2 reverse map. -/
232	private noncomputable def revInnerLeg (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
233	    (V : Opens X) (hxV : x ∈ V) :
234	    B.presheaf.obj (op V) ⟶ TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x :=
235	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom <|
236	    (ConcreteCategory.hom
237	        (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf (U ⊓ V) x ⟨hxU, hxV⟩)).comp
238	      (((TensorProduct.mk ↑(R.obj (op (U ⊓ V))) ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V))) ↑(B.obj (op (U ⊓ V)))
239	          ((ConcreteCategory.hom (A.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_left : U ⊓ V ⟶ U).op)) a :
240	            ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V))))).toAddMonoidHom).comp
241	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (B.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right : U ⊓ V ⟶ V).op)))
242	
243	/-- Evaluation of `revInnerLeg`: it sends `b` to the germ of `(a|_{U⊓V}) ⊗ (b|_{U⊓V})`. -/
244	private lemma revInnerLeg_apply (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
245	    (V : Opens X) (hxV : x ∈ V) (b : ↑(B.presheaf.obj (op V))) :
246	    (ConcreteCategory.hom (revInnerLeg A B x U hxU a V hxV)) b
247	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom
248	          (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf (U ⊓ V) x ⟨hxU, hxV⟩))
249	          (((ConcreteCategory.hom (A.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_left : U ⊓ V ⟶ U).op)) a :
250	              ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V)))) ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op (U ⊓ V))]
251	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom (B.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right : U ⊓ V ⟶ V).op)) b :
252	              ↑(B.obj (op (U ⊓ V))))) :=
253	  rfl
254	
255	/-- **Inner descent of the reverse map** (fixed `A`-section `a` over `U`): the additive
256	map `B.stalk x → (A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x` descending `revInnerLeg` through the `B`-stalk colimit. -/
257	private noncomputable def revInner (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) :
258	    TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x ⟶
259	      TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x :=
260	  colimit.desc ((OpenNhds.inclusion x).op ⋙ B.presheaf)
261	    ⟨TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x,
262	      fun W => revInnerLeg A B x U hxU a (unop W).1 (unop W).2,
263	      by
264	        intro W W' f
265	        apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
266	        ext b
267	        simp only [CategoryTheory.comp_apply, Functor.const_obj_map]
268	        erw [CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.id_apply, revInnerLeg_apply]
269	        conv_rhs => erw [revInnerLeg_apply]
270	        have hVle : (unop W').1 ≤ (unop W).1 := leOfHom ((OpenNhds.inclusion x).map f.unop)
271	        have j : U ⊓ (unop W').1 ⟶ U ⊓ (unop W).1 := homOfLE (inf_le_inf_left U hVle)
272	        rw [← TopCat.Presheaf.germ_res_apply (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf j x
273	          ⟨hxU, (unop W').2⟩]
274	        congr 1
275	        erw [presheaf_map_apply_coe, PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul]
276	        congr 1
277	        · rw [← presheaf_map_apply_coe, ← presheaf_map_apply_coe]
278	          erw [← CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← Functor.map_comp]
279	          congr 1
280	        · rw [← presheaf_map_apply_coe, Functor.comp_map]
281	          erw [← CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.comp_apply,
282	            ← CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← Functor.map_comp, ← Functor.map_comp]
283	          congr 1⟩
284	
285	/-- The inner descent factors `revInnerLeg` through the `B`-germ, by the colimit
286	universal property. -/
287	private lemma germ_revInner (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
288	    (V : Opens X) (hxV : x ∈ V) :
289	    TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf V x hxV ≫ revInner A B x U hxU a
290	      = revInnerLeg A B x U hxU a V hxV :=
291	  colimit.ι_desc _ (op (⟨V, hxV⟩ : OpenNhds x))
292	
293	/-- **Germ characterisation of the inner descent** on a section `b` over `V`: the inner
294	descent sends `germ b` to the germ of `(a|_{U⊓V}) ⊗ (b|_{U⊓V})`. -/
295	private lemma revInner_germ (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
296	    (V : Opens X) (hxV : x ∈ V) (b : ↑(B.presheaf.obj (op V))) :
297	    (ConcreteCategory.hom (revInner A B x U hxU a))
298	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf V x hxV)) b)
299	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom
300	          (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf (U ⊓ V) x ⟨hxU, hxV⟩))
301	          (((ConcreteCategory.hom (A.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_left : U ⊓ V ⟶ U).op)) a :
302	              ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V)))) ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op (U ⊓ V))]
303	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom (B.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right : U ⊓ V ⟶ V).op)) b :
304	              ↑(B.obj (op (U ⊓ V))))) := by
305	  rw [← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, germ_revInner]
306	  exact revInnerLeg_apply A B x U hxU a V hxV b
307	
308	/-- **Outer cocone leg for the reverse map.** For a neighbourhood `U ∋ x`, the additive map
309	`A(U) → (B.stalk x →+ (A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x)` sending an `A`-section `a` to the inner descent
310	`revInner` it determines. Additivity in `a` is checked on germ generators of `B.stalk x`
311	via `revInner_germ` and bilinearity of the section tensor. -/
312	private noncomputable def revOuterLeg (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) :
313	    (↑(A.obj (op U)) : Type u) →+
314	      ((↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x) : Type u) →+
315	        ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x)) where
316	  toFun a := ConcreteCategory.hom (revInner A B x U hxU a)
317	  map_add' a a' := by
318	    apply AddMonoidHom.ext
319	    intro ξ
320	    obtain ⟨V, hxV, b, rfl⟩ := TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist B.presheaf x ξ
321	    simp only [AddMonoidHom.add_apply, revInner_germ]
322	    erw [_root_.map_add, TensorProduct.add_tmul, _root_.map_add]
323	  map_zero' := by
324	    apply AddMonoidHom.ext
325	    intro ξ
326	    obtain ⟨V, hxV, b, rfl⟩ := TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist B.presheaf x ξ
327	    simp only [AddMonoidHom.zero_apply, revInner_germ]
328	    erw [_root_.map_zero, TensorProduct.zero_tmul, _root_.map_zero]
329	
330	/-- Evaluation of the outer leg: it is the inner descent. -/
331	private lemma revOuterLeg_apply (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
332	    (ξ : ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x)) :
333	    (revOuterLeg A B x U hxU a) ξ = (ConcreteCategory.hom (revInner A B x U hxU a)) ξ :=
334	  rfl
335	
336	/-- **Outer descent of the reverse map.** The additive map
337	`A.stalk x → (B.stalk x →+ (A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x)` descending `revOuterLeg` through the
338	`A`-stalk colimit; this is the additive bilinear comparison underlying the reverse map. -/
339	private noncomputable def revBihom :
340	    TopCat.Presheaf.stalk A.presheaf x ⟶
341	      AddCommGrpCat.of ((↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x) : Type u) →+
342	        ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x)) :=
343	  colimit.desc ((OpenNhds.inclusion x).op ⋙ A.presheaf)
344	    ⟨AddCommGrpCat.of _,
345	      fun U => AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (revOuterLeg A B x (unop U).1 (unop U).2),
346	      by
347	        intro W W' f
348	        apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
349	        ext a
350	        simp only [CategoryTheory.comp_apply, Functor.const_obj_map]
351	        erw [CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.id_apply]
352	        apply AddMonoidHom.ext
353	        intro ξ
354	        obtain ⟨V, hxV, b, rfl⟩ := TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist B.presheaf x ξ
355	        erw [revOuterLeg_apply, revOuterLeg_apply, revInner_germ, revInner_germ]
356	        have hVle : (unop W').1 ≤ (unop W).1 := leOfHom ((OpenNhds.inclusion x).map f.unop)
357	        have j : (unop W').1 ⊓ V ⟶ (unop W).1 ⊓ V := homOfLE (inf_le_inf_right V hVle)
358	        rw [← TopCat.Presheaf.germ_res_apply (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf j x
359	          ⟨(unop W').2, hxV⟩]
360	        congr 1
361	        erw [presheaf_map_apply_coe, PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul]
362	        congr 1
363	        · rw [← presheaf_map_apply_coe, ← presheaf_map_apply_coe, Functor.comp_map]
364	          erw [← CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.comp_apply,
365	            ← CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← Functor.map_comp, ← Functor.map_comp]
366	          congr 1
367	        · rw [← presheaf_map_apply_coe]
368	          erw [← CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← Functor.map_comp]
369	          congr 1⟩
370	
371	/-- The outer descent factors `revOuterLeg` through the `A`-germ. -/
372	private lemma germ_revBihom (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) :
373	    TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hxU ≫ revBihom A B x
374	      = AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (revOuterLeg A B x U hxU) :=
375	  colimit.ι_desc _ (op (⟨U, hxU⟩ : OpenNhds x))
376	
377	/-- **Germ characterisation of the additive bilinear comparison** on germ generators:
378	`revBihom (germ a) (germ b)` is the germ of `(a|_{U⊓V}) ⊗ (b|_{U⊓V})`. -/
379	private lemma revBihom_germ_tmul (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
380	    (V : Opens X) (hxV : x ∈ V) (b : ↑(B.obj (op V))) :
381	    ((ConcreteCategory.hom (revBihom A B x))
382	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hxU)) a))
383	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf V x hxV)) b)
384	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom
385	          (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf (U ⊓ V) x ⟨hxU, hxV⟩))
386	          (((ConcreteCategory.hom (A.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_left : U ⊓ V ⟶ U).op)) a :
387	              ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V)))) ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op (U ⊓ V))]
388	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom (B.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right : U ⊓ V ⟶ V).op)) b :
389	              ↑(B.obj (op (U ⊓ V))))) := by
390	  have hb : (ConcreteCategory.hom (revBihom A B x))
391	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hxU)) a)
392	      = revOuterLeg A B x U hxU a := by
393	    rw [← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, germ_revBihom]
394	    rfl
395	  rw [hb, revOuterLeg_apply, revInner_germ]

397	-- **Stage (iv) remaining gap — the `R.stalk x`-balancing `revBihom_balanced`.**
398	-- The balancing `revBihom (r • a) b = revBihom a (r • b)` (the condition feeding
399	-- `TensorProduct.liftAddHom` to produce `stalkTensorRev : A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x →+ St`) reduces,
400	-- on germ generators over a common neighbourhood `W := T ⊓ U ⊓ V`, via
401	--   `rw [hrW, haW, hbW, ← germ_smul A, ← germ_smul B, revBihom_germ_tmul, revBihom_germ_tmul]`
402	--   `congr 1`
403	-- to the section-level identity (in the section tensor over `W ⊓ W`)
404	--   `(A.map _ (r' • a')) ⊗ (B.map _ b') = (A.map _ a') ⊗ (B.map _ (r' • b'))`.
405	-- The germ reduction (`germ_smul`, `revBihom_germ_tmul`) all fire; the residual is a
406	-- *restrictScalars/carrier-duality* wall. `PresheafOfModules.map_smul` pulls the scalar
407	-- through the restriction, but the resulting smul lives over the `RingCat` carrier
408	-- `(R ⋙ forget₂).obj (op (W⊓W))` and on the `restrictScalars`-module
409	-- `(ModuleCat.restrictScalars _).obj (A.obj (op (W⊓W)))` — whereas the section tensor is
410	-- annotated over the `CommRingCat` carrier `R.obj (op (W⊓W))` on the plain module
411	-- `A.obj (op (W⊓W))`. Hence `TensorProduct.smul_tmul` fails to synthesize a `DistribMulAction`
412	-- defeq to the inferred `ModuleCat.instModuleCarrierObjRestrictScalars`.
413	-- NEXT PROVER ROUND: close this section identity with the over-ring `erw`/`smul_tmul'` recipe
414	-- (mirror `stalkTensorDescU_smul`, which solves the same `RingCat`-vs-`CommRingCat` section smul),
415	-- inserting the canonical `RingEquiv` bridge between the carriers as flagged in the blueprint
416	-- stage (iii) note; OR avoid the `W⊓W` restriction by proving the balancing at the stalk level
417	-- `revBihom (r • a) b = r • revBihom a b` through the `T`-presheaf `germ_smul`. Then define
418	--   `stalkTensorRev := TensorProduct.liftAddHom (ConcreteCategory.hom (revBihom A B x))
419	--      revBihom_balanced`.
420	-- All inputs (`revBihom`, `revBihom_germ_tmul`, `revOuterLeg_apply`) are built axiom-clean above.
421	
422	end StalkTensor

8	/-!
9	# Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf (`i = 0`)
10	
11	This file establishes the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of flat base change: the
12	formation of the pushforward `f_* F` of a quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat
13	base change on the target.
14	
15	Throughout we work with a (commutative, eventually cartesian) square of schemes
16	```
17	  X' --g'--> X
18	  |f'        |f
19	  v          v
20	  S' --g---> S
21	```
22	recorded by morphisms `f : X ⟶ S`, `g : S' ⟶ S`, `f' : X' ⟶ S'`, `g' : X' ⟶ X`
23	with `g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`, and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of modules on `X`.
24	
25	The three main declarations are:
26	
27	* `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap` — the canonical base-change map
28	  `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)`, built as the adjoint mate of the unit of the
29	  `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction.
30	* `AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` — for `f` affine and the
31	  square cartesian, the base-change map is an isomorphism (affine case: tensor
32	  associativity).
33	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` — for `g` flat and `f`
34	  quasi-compact quasi-separated, the base-change map is an isomorphism.
35	
36	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`.
37	
38	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
39	Tag 02KH.
40	-/
41	
42	universe u
43	
44	open CategoryTheory Limits
45	
46	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
47	
48	open Scheme.Modules
49	
50	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
51	  (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
52	
53	/-- The canonical base-change map for the pushforward of a sheaf of modules.
54	
55	Given a commutative square
56	```
57	  X' --g'--> X
58	  |f'        |f
59	  v          v
60	  S' --g---> S
61	```
62	(with `comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`) and a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`, this is the
63	canonical morphism `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)` of sheaves of modules on `S'`.
64	
65	It is the image, under the `(g^*, g_*)`-adjunction transpose, of the composite
66	```
67	  f_* F --f_*(unit)--> f_* (g')_* (g')^* F
68	        = (g' ≫ f)_* (g')^* F = (f' ≫ g)_* (g')^* F = g_* f'_* (g')^* F,
69	```
70	where `unit` is the unit of the `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction and the middle
71	equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward together with the
72	commutativity of the square.
73	
74	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
75	base-change diagram. -/
76	noncomputable def pushforwardBaseChangeMap (comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g) (F : X.Modules) :
77	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj ((pushforward f).obj F) ⟶
78	      (pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) :=
79	  ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).homEquiv _ _).symm
80	    ((pushforward f).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g').unit.app F) ≫
81	      (pushforwardComp g' f).hom.app _ ≫
82	      (pushforwardCongr comm).hom.app _ ≫
83	      (pushforwardComp f' g).inv.app _)
84	
85	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — locality of isomorphisms for `Scheme.Modules`
86	
87	Mathlib provides the per-open criterion `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`
88	(`IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U)`) and the stalkwise criterion
89	`TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso` for `TopCat.Sheaf`-valued morphisms,
90	but it does not package the stalk-local criterion at the level of `Scheme.Modules`
91	morphisms. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they are the locality tools needed for
92	the affine reduction of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (where one checks the
93	base-change map after restricting to / taking stalks over affine opens). -/
94	
95	/-- **Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** A morphism `φ : M ⟶ N`
96	of sheaves of modules over a scheme `X` is an isomorphism iff its underlying morphism of
97	abelian presheaves is a stalkwise isomorphism. Project-local: Mathlib only ships the
98	per-open criterion and the `TopCat.Sheaf`-level stalk criterion separately. -/
99	theorem Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
100	    (φ : M ⟶ N) :
101	    IsIso φ ↔ ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map
102	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) := by
103	  constructor
104	  · intro h x
105	    haveI : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) := Functor.map_isIso _ _
106	    exact Functor.map_isIso _ _
107	  · intro h
108	    -- Package the underlying abelian presheaves as `TopCat.Sheaf`es.
109	    let MS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩
110	    let NS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨N.presheaf, N.isSheaf⟩
111	    let fS : MS ⟶ NS := ⟨(Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ⟩
112	    haveI : ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map fS.hom) := h
113	    haveI hSiso : IsIso fS := TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso fS
114	    have h1 : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) :=
115	      (TopCat.Sheaf.forget Ab.{u} X).map_isIso fS
116	    exact (CategoryTheory.isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso φ (Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X)).mp h1
117	
118	/-- **Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** If `B` is a basis of
119	opens of `X` and `φ : M ⟶ N` restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every basic
120	open `B i`, then `φ` is an isomorphism. This reduces iso-checking from *all* opens (the
121	content of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`) to a chosen basis. Project-local:
122	Mathlib provides the stalkwise pieces (`germ_exist_of_isBasis`,
123	`stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis`) but not the packaged criterion at the
124	`Scheme.Modules` level. -/
125	theorem Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
126	    {ι : Type*} {B : ι → X.Opens} (hB : TopologicalSpace.Opens.IsBasis (Set.range B))
127	    (φ : M ⟶ N) (h : ∀ i, IsIso (φ.app (B i))) : IsIso φ := by
128	  -- Reduce to a stalkwise isomorphism of the underlying `Ab`-presheaf morphism `α`.
129	  rw [Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map]
130	  intro x
131	  -- `α.app (op (B i))` is definitionally `φ.app (B i)`, hence an isomorphism on each basic open.
132	  have happ : ∀ U ∈ Set.range B,
133	      IsIso (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U)) := by
134	    rintro U ⟨i, rfl⟩; exact h i
135	  rw [CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]
136	  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
137	  · -- Injectivity of the stalk map from injectivity on a basis.
138	    refine TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis hB ?_ x
139	    intro U hU
140	    haveI := happ U hU
141	    exact (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso
142	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).injective
143	  · -- Surjectivity: a germ at `x` comes from a section over a basic open, where `α` is onto.
144	    intro t
145	    obtain ⟨U, hxU, hU, s, rfl⟩ :=
146	      TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist_of_isBasis hB N.presheaf x t
147	    haveI := happ U hU
148	    obtain ⟨s', hs'⟩ := (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso
149	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).surjective s
150	    refine ⟨M.presheaf.germ U x hxU s', ?_⟩
151	    erw [TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_germ_apply]
152	    rw [hs']
153	    rfl

155	/-- **Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** A morphism
156	`φ : M ⟶ N` of sheaves of modules is an isomorphism iff it restricts to an isomorphism on
157	the sections over every affine open of `X`. This is the precise reduction used in the
158	affine proof of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso`: the affine opens of `X` form a basis,
159	and over an affine open the base-change map becomes the pure ring-theoretic
160	`cancelBaseChange` isomorphism. -/
161	theorem Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
162	    (φ : M ⟶ N) : IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U : X.affineOpens, IsIso (φ.app U) := by
163	  refine ⟨fun _ U => inferInstance, fun h => ?_⟩

165	    (B := (Subtype.val : X.affineOpens → X.Opens))
